title: Sophia Stel Doesn't Need a Script — A24's First Music Signing Is Writing Her Own
subtitle: The Vancouver producer turned A24's inaugural signee makes music that sounds like walking home alone and being completely fine with it.
author: Allastair Voss
body:
[spotify:artist/18w9tq3c2x11niEFNYqeex]

When A24 announced it was moving into music, the question everyone asked was: who? The studio that made grief beautiful in Moonlight, made chaos elegant in Everything Everywhere All at Once, and made Midsommar somehow the feel-good horror film of a generation, who would they trust with the sonic equivalent of that vision? The answer was Sophia Stel. A 24-year-old from Vancouver who records in the basement of a DIY venue. That choice tells you everything you need to know about where A24 thinks music is going. They went looking for someone serious and found her in a basement.

[youtube:L-14HNoSrV4]

## Solitaire Rules

The name of her second EP, How to Win at Solitaire, came from a moment that is almost too perfect as metaphor. Stel googled how to play the card game and found nothing useful, vague advice, no guaranteed outcome, just the sense that you have to figure it out yourself. That is her music in a sentence. It does not arrive with instructions. It does not tell you how to feel. It sits next to you and waits.

The original EP was written, produced, and recorded entirely by Stel in the basement of Paradise, a DIY venue in Vancouver where she was essentially living and working. The Deluxe Edition, released earlier this year on Pack Records, adds three reworked tracks that expand the project without betraying it. Mura Masa strips "I'll Take It" into an acoustic-rock space that somehow makes the song feel both bigger and more intimate. Tommy Genesis shows up on "All My Friends Are Models" with a verse that should clash with Stel's restraint but instead locks in perfectly. Cecile Believe, one of SOPHIE's closest collaborators, pushes "Everyone Falls Asleep In Their Own Time" toward an art-pop euphoria that keeps the original's melancholy intact.

The production on the original EP is the first thing to understand about what Stel is doing. Working alone in a basement, she developed a sound that is neither polished nor deliberately lo-fi, that sits in a zone of intentional texture where the room itself becomes part of the recording. You can hear the space in these songs. Not as a flaw but as a choice, the acoustics of a particular basement in a particular city contributing something that a commercial studio would eliminate. This is a specific and rare kind of production intelligence: knowing what to keep rather than what to add.

## The A24 of It All

There is a reason this partnership works, and it is not just branding. A24 has always been drawn to artists who make you uncomfortable in productive ways, who leave gaps in the narrative for you to fill. Stel does exactly that with music. Her production is minimal but never empty. Dreamy synths layer over lyrics that offer observation rather than resolution. She has been compared to Ethel Cain, FKA twigs, and Imogen Heap, and all three references land without fully capturing what she does.

There is something cinematic about her songs, but it is the cinema of small moments. Not the score to the car chase but the sound of the drive home after. A24 films occupy that same register. They are interested in interiority, in the gap between what characters say and what they mean, in the weight of ordinary decisions. Stel's music inhabits that gap. The lyrics do not explain the emotional content. The emotional content is present in the production, in the pacing, in the space around the vocal.

What the A24 co-sign actually does is give her an audience that already knows how to listen to work that trusts them. A24's audience has been trained by years of films that do not over-explain. They are comfortable with ambiguity. They are the right audience for Sophia Stel. That alignment is not accidental. It is the result of someone at A24 understanding exactly what this music is doing and who it is doing it for. The institutional fit is genuine.

## The Collaborations and What They Prove

The Deluxe Edition is worth examining closely because of what the collaborations reveal about the original material. When Mura Masa strips "I'll Take It" down to acoustic-rock, he is not changing the song. He is revealing a structure that was always there, demonstrating that the song is strong enough to survive a complete change of production approach. That is a rigorous test of songwriting. The song passes.

The Tommy Genesis feature on "All My Friends Are Models" is the boldest choice on the deluxe and also the most revealing. Genesis's presence on a Sophia Stel track is a genuine juxtaposition, two artists operating in different registers with different relationships to restraint. That it works says something about both artists, about Genesis's range and about Stel's material being more capacious than its restraint suggests.

Cecile Believe is the collaborator who makes the most sense on paper and delivers the most surprising result. Her SOPHIE connection brings a history of art-pop maximalism to Stel's minimalism, and "Everyone Falls Asleep In Their Own Time" becomes something you could not have predicted from the original but that feels inevitable once you hear it. The collaboration extends the EP's emotional range without changing its fundamental character. Three collaborators, three very different interventions, all of them successful. That is a strong argument for the strength of the underlying songwriting.

## What Comes Next

Her debut full-length album is due this year, and her Europe and North America tour kicks off today, April 10. The timing of the tour launch aligning with this moment feels intentional. Stel is building something slow and deliberate, the way her songs build. No viral TikTok strategy. No feature-stacking for playlist placement. Just the music, the basement, the quiet ambition of someone who knows the game is solitaire and is fine playing it alone.

The question is not whether Sophia Stel will break through. She is already doing it. The question is whether the industry will understand what she is doing before she outgrows its categories entirely. A24 understood early. The rest of us are catching up.
